title: The Unnamed Register
subtitle: {key:""} — where every yank and delete lands by default.
summary: Every yank and delete writes the unnamed register. {key:p} and {key:P} read from it. It is the implicit clipboard of every Vim editing session.

blocks.text: When you press {key:y}, {key:d}, {key:c}, or {key:x}, the affected text goes into the **unnamed register**. When you press {key:p} or {key:P}, the unnamed register comes back. Unless you say otherwise, this is *the* register your editing uses.

blocks.caption: Yank, delete, paste — all use the unnamed register

blocks.title: It Has a Name: {key:"}{key:"}
blocks.text:
Despite the name, the unnamed register is reachable: {key:"}{key:"}{key:p} explicitly pastes from it. So {key:"}{key:"} is a register name like any other; "unnamed" just means it's the *default*.

Every one of these commands writes it:

  • Yank: {key:y}{motion}, {key:y}{key:y}, {key:Y}.
  • Delete: {key:d}{motion}, {key:d}{key:d}, {key:D}, {key:x}, {key:X}.
  • Change: {key:c}{motion}, {key:c}{key:c}, {key:C}, {key:s}, {key:S}.
  • Substitute: {key:r}{c} *does not* write the unnamed register (the original character is in the small-delete register {key:"}{key:-} instead).

And these read it: {key:p}, {key:P}, {key:g}{key:p}, {key:g}{key:P}, {key:]}{key:p}, {key:[}{key:p}.

Knowing this is useful because it explains *why* paste sometimes shows you the wrong text — you yanked something, then deleted something, and the delete clobbered the unnamed. The yank is preserved in {key:"}{key:0} (the yank register); the latest delete is in {key:"}{key:-} (small-delete) or {key:"}{key:1} (numbered).

If you want pasting to use the system clipboard instead, set `:set clipboard=unnamedplus` (Linux) or `unnamed` (macOS). That makes the unnamed register *be* the clipboard. See [*System Clipboard Registers*](#registers.clipboard).

blocks.title: {key:"}{key:"} is the last-write bus
blocks.text: Every yank, delete, and change writes the unnamed register in addition to whatever explicit register you named. So even a stray {key:x} can clobber a previous yank. If you want to preserve a yank across a delete, route the delete to the black-hole register: {key:"}{key:_}{key:d}{key:d}.
